coredata.dc:title: Aldehyde modified nanocellulose-based fluorescent hydrogel toward multistage data security encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Sijie
Zhong, Han
Wang, Zhiguo
Lv, Haifeng
Jiang, Jianchun
Pu, Junwen
coredata.dc:description: In view of the insecurity of encode information storage based on fluorescence switch single-stage encryption, a fluorescent hydrogel for multistage data security encryption were proposed, named as polyvinyl alcohol/dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils/carbon quantum dots hydrogel. Herein, the interpenetrating network was formed by chemically crosslinking between polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils (DACNF). Additionally, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method were introduced into the above hydrogel system by hydrogen bonds. The resultant fluorescent hydrogels possessed high stretchability up to 530 %, good strength of 0.96 MPa, Fe3+-responsive fluorescence quenching, fluorescence recovery triggered by ascorbic acid and borax-triggered shape memory. Moreover, various complex 3D hydrogel geometries were fabricated by folding/assembling 2D fluorescent hydrogel sheets, extending data encryption capability from 2D plane to 3D space. More remarkably, the 3D data encryption-erasing process of fluorescent hydrogel was realized by the strategy of alternating treatment of Fe3+ solution and ascorbic acid solution. This work provided a facile and general strategy for constructing high security important information encryption and protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent hydrogel
Aldehyde cellulose nanofibrils
Data encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots
The preparation of PVA/DACNF/CDs fluorescent hydrogel

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Special Fund for Beijing Common Construction Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was sponsored by the Scientific Research Funds of Huaqiao University ( 605-50Y22069 ), and Special Fund for Beijing Common Construction Project , Grant No. 2016HXKFCLXY0015 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of preparing PDC fluorescent hydrogels. (b) TEM images of DACNF.

(a) FT-IR absorption spectrum of DACNF and CDs. (b) TEM images of CDs. (c) SEM images of PD and PDC hydrogels. (d) XRD patterns of CNF, DACNF and CDs. (e) UV/Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs in aqueous solutions.

(a) Tensile stress-strain curves of PD hydrogels with different additions of DACNF. (b) Swelling ratio of PD hydrogels with different additions of DACNF. (c) Cyclic tensile stress-strain curves of PDC hydrogel under different strains. (d) The dissipated energy and the energy dissipation ratio of PDC hydrogel with four times under different strains. (e) Cyclic tensile stress-strain curves of PDC hydrogel at 100 % strain and (f) the corresponding energy dissipation.

(a) Shape memory illustration of PD hydrogel triggered by borax. (b) Shape fixity ratios of the hydrogels with different additions of DACNF. (c) The relationship between shape fixity ratios and time of PD-1.5 hydrogel. (d) Shape recovery ratios of PD-1.5 hydrogel in hydrochloric acid solution.
Complex 3D PVA/DACNF hydrogel geometries including (a) pentagon splice, (b) bowl-shaped hydrogel, pinwheel and crane-shaped hydrogel. (e) The cyclic test on shape memory of PVA/DACNF hydrogel.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of PDC hydrogel at different pH (pH = 2, 4, 7, 9 and 11). (b) Fluorescence spectra of PDC hydrogel before and after being treated with 0.1 mol/L Fe3+ solution.

(a) Photographs of PDC hydrogels under visible (left) and UV (right) light. (b) Images of PDC hydrogels before and after ion-printing by using Fe3+ as ink under visible light. 2D fluorescent hydrogel information storage printed with (c) “CN” characters and (d) the special logo.
Fluorescent hydrogel is stored with “CNFU” and “1C2H3Z” and folded into 3D geometries (a) tetrahedron and (b) regular cube. (c) Data decryption of 3D hydrogel geometries.
(a) Information erasure and (b) writing-erasure cycle test of PDC fluorescent hydrogel.

head.ce:title: Aldehyde modified nanocellulose-based fluorescent hydrogel toward multistage data security encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Advanced Carbon Conversion Technology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Biomass Low-Carbon Conversion, Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Advanced Carbon Conversion Technology
Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Biomass Low-Carbon Conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Advanced Carbon Conversion Technology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Biomass Low-Carbon Conversion, Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry, MOE Engineering Research Center of Forestry Biomass Materials and Bioenergy, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry
MOE Engineering Research Center of Forestry Biomass Materials and Bioenergy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry, MOE Engineering Research Center of Forestry Biomass Materials and Bioenergy, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In view of the insecurity of encode information storage based on fluorescence switch single-stage encryption, a fluorescent hydrogel for multistage data security encryption were proposed, named as polyvinyl alcohol/dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils/carbon quantum dots hydrogel. Herein, the interpenetrating network was formed by chemically crosslinking between polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils (DACNF). Additionally, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method were introduced into the above hydrogel system by hydrogen bonds. The resultant fluorescent hydrogels possessed high stretchability up to 530 %, good strength of 0.96 MPa, Fe3+-responsive fluorescence quenching, fluorescence recovery triggered by ascorbic acid and borax-triggered shape memory. Moreover, various complex 3D hydrogel geometries were fabricated by folding/assembling 2D fluorescent hydrogel sheets, extending data encryption capability from 2D plane to 3D space. More remarkably, the 3D data encryption-erasing process of fluorescent hydrogel was realized by the strategy of alternating treatment of Fe3+ solution and ascorbic acid solution. This work provided a facile and general strategy for constructing high security important information encryption and protection.
The polyvinyl alcohol/dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils/carbon quantum dots hydrogel possessed Fe3+-responsive fluorescence quenching, fluorescence recovery triggered by ascorbic

acid and borax-triggered shape memory, which could be used for 3D data security encryption.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Data storage and encryption are frequently employed in economic and military fields, particularly to commercial products, currencies, and certificates [1]. Photoluminescent materials, which fluoresce under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, have been widely employed to generate fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting products, such as organic dyes, lanthanide-doped luminescent materials, and semiconductor nanomaterials [2,3]. However, these materials continue to be plagued by expensive price, complex preparation techniques, high toxicity, and poor photostability. Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as novel carbon nanomaterials following fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and graphene, have gained a great deal of attention for bioimaging, anti-counterfeiting, catalysis, and sensing applications owing to excellent fluorescence emission stability, biocompatibility, and photobleaching resistance [4]. Long et al. designed fluorine and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with blue pH-stabilized fluorescence, which could be acted as a smart concealed ink for information encryption and decryption [5]. Kalytchuk and colleagues demonstrated a time-coded system in the nanosecond range for anti-counterfeiting labels utilizing two different sets of CDs as ink [6]. However, the fluorescence of CDs will be significantly reduced or even disappeared due to aggregation quenching in dry powder state, which profoundly limits the application of CDs.

Bhattacharya et al. constructed multicolor and fluorescent self-healing hydrogels through Schiff base reaction between CDs loading with aldehyde units and polyethylenimine [7]. Wei et al. reported a smart wearable hydrogel sensor with photostress-pH multi-responsiveness based on CDs, chitosan quaternary and carboxymethylcellulose [8]. These reports suggested that introducing CDs into hydrogels with three-dimensional cross-linked networks not only solves the problem of fluorescence quenching, but also endows hydrogels with optical property and physical support in mechanical property. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a common water soluble polyhydroxyl polymer, usually requires the introduction of a cross-linked network to form a hydrogel for achieving several application [9]. It is widely known the PVA hydrogel prepared by the simple cyclic freezing-thawing method shows thermal instability and poor mechanical properties. Traditional chemical crosslinking agent molecules can improve the above problems, but the potential toxicity of residual molecules still limits the application of hydrogels. Therefore, it is of great significance for the practical application of PVA-based hydrogels to select low toxicity biological crosslinkers instead of low molecular weight chemical crosslinkers. Dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils (DACNFs) with two adjacent aldehyde groups in the single repetitive glucose unit, is prepared from selective oxidation of cellulose nanofibrils by sodium periodate. In virtue of its high crystallinity, nanometer size effect, low toxicity and biocompatibility, it has been widely used as the reinforcement and crosslinking agent of polymer hydrogel [10]. Nevertheless, this single-stage encrypted hydrogels based on fluorescent switches can be broken by certain chemical methods. Therefore, realizing efficient multistage data encryption is necessary to enhance the complexity of decryption.

Recently, inspired by the unique features of reversible deformation or color change by external stimulation, intelligent hydrogels have attracted a great deal of attention in data storage and encryption. Stimuli-responsive hydrogels make it possible to transform data storage and encryption from traditional 2D planar to 3D space in order to further enhance information security level. For example, Liao and coworkers prepared a photoactive hydrogel based on the cracking of hydrogel chemical crosslinking network induced by ultraviolet light [11]. It successfully achieved the storage of 3D information at the macro/micro scales. Le et al. realized multi-level and multi-dimensional information storage and encryption of fluorescent hydrogels by combining Fe3+ ion dyeing and printing method with the formation and breaking of metal coordination bonds [12]. In addition, polymeric hydrogel with thermo-triggered polymorphic fluorescence switching, shape memory, and self-healing capabilities was also prepared by combining aggregation-induced emissive mechanism with the dynamic lanthanide coordination interactions strategy, achieving 3D spatial data coding [13]. None of these fluorescent messages with 3D encryption method can be read directly. Only through the preset decryption procedure can the encrypted information be read correctly, thereby increasing the difficulty of data encryption/decryption. Inspired by these impressive ideas, we envisioned 3D origami geometries based on fluorescent and shape memory properties for multistage date encryption. As far as we know, to date, very few studies, have used these kinds of PVA/DACNF/CDs as hydrogels for 3D data security encryption.

In this work, DACNF prepared by sodium periodate oxidation and CDs synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method, were introduced into PVA solution to develop PVA/DACNF/CDs shape memorized fluorescent hydrogel. The fluorescent hydrogel showed good mechanical strength and high tensile strain. In addition, based on visible Fe3+-controlled fluorescence quenching of CDs, using Fe3+ as “ink” to write data into hydrogel films was possible. Moreover, the fluorescent hydrogels exhibited shape memory triggered by borax, and 3D data encryption/decryption was achieved by further folding and assembling hydrogels. In this way, the encrypted data can be read only after the shape recovery and ultraviolet irradiation, indicating significantly enhanced information security compared with traditional 2D counterparts. Besides, the encrypted data could be erased by ascorbic acid treatment due to strong reducing action. This simple and general strategy provides an idea for enriching information storage and protection patterns.

Ethylenediamine (EDA), citric acid and sodium tetrabotrate were of analytical grade and purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (China). Cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) were supplied by Tianjin Woodelf Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with number-average polymerization degree of 1799, sodium periodate (NaIO4) and ascorbic acid were offered by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). And all reagents were used without further treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized according to the method reported with slightly modification [14]. In brief, 5 g of citric acid and 6 mL of ethylenediamine were added to 20 mL of deionized water with ultrasonic dispersing for 30 min. Then the above-mentioned mixture was brought up a temperature of 180 °C for 5 h by one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the prepared CDs were filtered through 0.22 μm filter, and dialyzed against deionized water for 3 days to remove the incomplete reactants. Finally, the CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying method.

Dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils were prepared according to the method described previously [15]. Typically, known amount CNFs and 9 mmol sodium periodate (per gram of cellulose nanofibrils) were mixed at 45 °C for 24 h, shielded from light. After the reaction was finished, excessive ethylene glycol was added to terminate the reaction. Finally, the crude product was repeatedly centrifugal washing with deionized water and ethanol to obtain the purified DACNFs.

Initially, 6.5 wt% PVA and the certain amount (0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 wt%) of DACNF were dispersed into 20 mL deionized water, and stirred at 95 °C for 1.5 h to achieve a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, CDs powder with a final concentration of 5 mg/L, and 1 mol/L hydrochloric acid solution were added to the above solution, continuously mixing for 3 h at 70 °C. The mixture was transferred to a mold, followed by nature cooling and cross-linked solidification in a dryer until slowly forming a gel. Finally, the composite hydrogel was purified and washed with deionized water to remove the hydrochloric acid solution. The obtained PVA/DACNF/CDs fluorescent hydrogels with different DACNF content were named as PDC-R hydrogel, where R represents the addition of DACNF. For comparison, PVA/DACNF hydrogel without carbon quantum dots was prepared using the same method and coded as PD-R hydrogel.

The surface observation of the freeze-drying hydrogels was recorded using field emission-scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Gemini 300, Germany). The transmission electron microscopic measurement (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, USA) was conducted to investigate the CDs microstructure. The chemical structure of the samples was confirmed by a ThermoNicolet 300 Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (USA) in the range of 500–4000 cm−1. The UV–Vis spectrum of CDs was measured using Agilent Cary-100 UV–Vis absorption spectrometer (USA) with a scanning range of 200–700 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra were determined by FLS1000 steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (UK) with a scan region between 250 and 600 nm. The crystalline structure of samples was obtained by Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer (Japan), recording with the diffraction angle (2θ) range from 5° to 60°. And the crystallinity of DACNFs (Xc) was calculated by Segal empirical method.
(1)
X c
=
I 200
−
I am
I 200
× 100 %
where I200 and Iam denote the diffraction intensity of (200) lattice planes and amorphous region, respectively.

The uniaxial tensile tests of composite hydrogels were measured with a universal testing machine (Zwick 1.0, Germany) at room temperature. The measurement was performed three times for each sample. The toughness (T) was calculated based on the area under the stress-strain curve, while Young's modulus (Y) was calculated from the elastic region of the stress–strain curve. The energy dissipation (ΔE) for the loading-unloading test was estimated by the hysteresis area contained in the stress-strain curve. And the energy loss ratio (δ) was used to evaluate the energy dissipation efficiency of hydrogels. These calculation formulas are as follows:
(2)
T = ∫ σ ⅆ μ
(3)
Y =
σ μ
μ < 15 %
(4)
ΔE =
∫ loading
σ ⅆ μ −
∫ unloading
σ ⅆ μ
(5)
δ =
ΔE E
where σ, μ and E represent the stress, strain and stored energy at loading, respectively.

pH-dependent fluorescence test: the PDC hydrogel was immersed in aqueous solutions with different pH values (pH = 2, 4, 7, 9, 11) for 3 h, and then the fluorescence spectrum of different hydrogels was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

Shape memory and recovery test: the PD hydrogels were cut into straight strips with 50 mm length × 3 mm width × 1 mm thickness. Then U-shaped hydrogel deformed by an external load was soaked in 0.1 mol/L borax solution for 10 min. And shape recovery process was performed in hydrochloric acid solution. To quantitatively describe the shape memory and recovery effect of PD hydrogels, the rate of fixation (Rf) and recovery (Rr) were evaluated using following equations:
(6)
R f
=
180 −
θ 2
180 −
θ 1
× 100 %
(7)
R r
=
θ 3
180
× 100 %
where θ
1 , θ
2 and θ
3 denote the initial angle, the fixed angle and the recovered angle, respectively.

The equilibrium swelling rate (SR) test: the certain amount of hydrogel sample was completely immersed in deionized water, swelling to equilibrium at room temperature. Subsequently, the water on the hydrogels surface was wiped off by filter paper and weighed. Finally, it was further dried to a constant weight. The swelling rate was calculated as follow:
(8)
SR =
w 1
−
w 2
∕
w 2
where W1 and W2 are the weight of hydrogels before and after swelling, respectively.

Information storage experiment of the fluorescent hydrogels: the filter paper cut into a specific shape is soaked with Fe3+ ions solution, and then placed onto the surface of the as-prepared hydrogel for 15 s. During this period, Fe3+ ions spontaneously diffused into the hydrogel and quenched the fluorescence. After peeling the filter paper off, the information was simply transferred to the fluorescent hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of PVA/DACNF/CDs fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The PDC fluorescent hydrogel preparation procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1 , including the preparation of DACNF by sodium periodate oxidation, the synthesis of N-doped CDs by one-step hydrothermal method and the preparation of fluorescent composite hydrogels. The hydrothermal method, considered as one of the effective strategies for preparing CDs, not only realizes incomplete carbonization of carbon source, but also helps to retain more functional groups on the surface of CDs. Additionally, studies have claimed that the incorporation of atom N into carbon skeleton materials further improved the fluorescence intensity of CDs owing to similar atomic radius [16]. Therefore, N-doped CDs were fabricated using citric acid originated from plants and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources respectively. As shown in Fig. 1, sodium periodate has a high selectivity for the oxidation of CNF. In this reaction, C2-C3 bond of glucoside ring was broken and the dihydroxyl group at C2 and C3 positions was transformed into dialdehyde group [17]. DACNF could serve as a natural polymer crosslinker for the preparation of crosslinked network structure with PVA by acetal reaction under acids catalyst at 70 °C. The content of aldehyde group in DACNF was determined to be 2.842 mmol/g by hydroxylamine hydrochloride method [18]. Furthermore, compared with CNF, the diameter of DACNF increased from 30 nm to 35–45 nm (Figs. 1 and S1). It could be due to the addition of sodium periodate resulting in the break of lots of the intermolecular hydrogen bonds, thereby leading to the oxidation and dissolution of small diameter CNF in aqueous solution [19]. Due to the hydrogen bond interaction, CDs could be evenly dispersed in the hydrogel, ensuring the stable fluorescence characteristics of the composite hydrogel.

The typical FT-IR spectra for DACNF, CDs, PVA, PD hydrogel and PDC hydrogel are depicted in Figs. 2a and S1. The characteristic band of DACNF attributed to the carboxyl groups at 1600–1700 cm−1, the C-O-C stretching vibration at 1056 cm−1 and the CO stretching vibration of the aldehyde group at 1739 cm−1 could be clearly observed in Fig. 2a [20]. As for CDs, the peak at 3280 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1 were caused by OH stretching vibration and CH stretching vibration, respectively. The peak at 1657 cm−1 was associated with the CO stretching vibration, and the peaks belonged to the NH bending vibration and asymmetric C-NH-C stretching vibration were appeared at 1562 cm−1 and 1126 cm−1, respectively [21]. These results confirmed that the surface of prepared CDs possessed a large number of different functional groups. Compared with the spectrum of PVA, PD hydrogel appeared the C-O-C ether bond absorption peak at 1019 cm−1, and the intensity and shape of characteristic peak corresponding to hydroxyl stretching vibration was decreased and narrower (Fig. S2, Supporting information). It is due to the acetal reaction of the hydroxyl group of PVA with the aldehyde group of DACNF catalyzed by the acid. In addition, the absorption peak intensity of PD hydrogel at 1143 cm−1 was lower that of PVA, indicating the formation of PVA crystals was hindered by the crosslinking between PVA and DACNF [22]. PDC hydrogel showed similar infrared spectrum to PD hydrogel, accompanied by the decreased peak intensity at 1143 cm−1 due to the addition of CDs. Meanwhile, the hydroxyl stretching vibration peak shifted to a lower frequency, indicating hydrogen bond interaction between CDs and PD hydrogel.

According to the TEM images shown in Fig. 2b, the well-distributed carbon quantum dots (CDs) were observed spherical nanoparticles with non-uniform sizes and diameters of 2–6 nm. From higher-resolution transmission electron microscopy images, most CDs were amorphous particles without any lattice. Rare particles possessed distinguishable and regular lattice fringes with the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [19]. Additionally, PD hydrogel presented a typical porous network structure, and no obvious phase separation was observed in Fig. 2c. Compared with PD hydrogel, the pore structure of PDC hydrogel became denser as a result of the increased crosslinking density caused by the formation of hydrogen bonds. This dense structure could endow hydrogels better mechanical properties. The XRD patterns of DACNF and CDs were displayed in Fig. 2d. From the figure, DACNF and CNF retained the characteristic peaks of cellulose-I in 15.3°, 22.6°, and 34.7°, affiliated to (110), (200), and (004) lattice planes, respectively. According to the Segal empirical method, the crystallinity of DACNF reduced from 45.72 % to 34.48 % (Fig. 2d). This result indicated that the crystalline structure of cellulose nanofibers was unchanged but with a lower crystallinity after sodium periodate oxidation [23]. And CDs showed a broad peak centered at 25°, corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of highly disordered carbon-based materials [24]. It further illustrated that the synthesized CDs mainly existed in the form of amorphous carbon structures without complete and continuous crystalline structure.

As presented in Fig. 2e, fluorescence spectrum and UV–Vis absorption spectrum are important means to characterize carbon quantum dots, which can reflect the potential applications of CDs in fluorescent materials to a large extent. From the figure, the UV–Vis absorption peaks of CDs were focused on 240 nm and 350 nm, attributed to the π → π* transition of CC conjugated structure and n → π* transition originating from CO, respectively [25]. In the fluorescence spectrum, CDs had optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 360 nm and 450 nm respectively, resulting in distinct Stokes shift. Furthermore, it could be observed that CDs aqueous solution is yellow and transparent in broad daylight, but emitted a light blue fluorescence under a hand-held UV lamp (Fig. S3, Supporting information), showing fascinating fluorescence properties.

In addition, the mechanical properties of PD hydrogel and PDC hydrogel were investigated in Fig. 3 . It could be seen from the figure that when DACNF concentration increased from 0.5 wt% to 1.5 wt%, the fracture stress of PD hydrogel increased from 0.31 MPa to 0.96 MPa accompanied by elongation at break from 380 % to 530 % (Fig. 3a). This might be related to the synergistic effect of chemical crosslinking and hydrogen bonding between PVA and DACNF. With the addition of DACNF further increased to 2.0 wt%, the tensile stress of the hydrogel increased to 1.13 MPa but with an evident decrease in stretchability to 428 %. This phenomenon is consistent with recent research results [26], namely, continuous addition of DACNF increased the stress of the hydrogel, while the peak of elongation has been reached at a lower addition. Consequently, the toughness of PD hydrogels exhibited a similar trend, initially ascending and then descending with a peak at 2.464 MJ/m3. However, Young's modulus showed a decreasing trend with the addition of DACNF (Fig. S4, Supporting information). Hydrogels are porous and soft materials with high water-swellable property. It has been reported that the swelling capacity of hydrogels tended to be inversely proportional to the crosslinking density [27]. Therefore, the equilibrium swelling ratio of PD hydrogel could reflect the state of the cross-linked network. As shown in Fig. 3b, the SR of PD hydrogel of 0.5 wt%, 1.0 wt%, 1.5 wt%, and 2.0 wt% were 11.25, 9.32, 8.35, and 7.14, respectively. It is easy to see that the SR of the hydrogels gradually decreased with the increase in the amount of the DACNF crosslinking agent, indicating the internal structure of hydrogels became denser. This result further demonstrated that cross-linking between DACNF and PVA could improve the mechanical properties of hydrogels. Moreover, owing to the hydrogen bonding and nanoscale effect of CDs, the stress of PDC hydrogel exhibited better mechanical properties as shown in Fig. S5 (Supporting information). The stress-strain curves of PDC hydrogel for 4 consecutive stretching cycles under different elongations were displayed in Fig. 3c. Correspondingly, the dissipated energy and the energy dissipation ratio of 4 cyclic exhibited in Fig. 3d. From the figure, the loading-unloading curves of PDC hydrogel under different elongations showed obvious hysteresis phenomenon, presenting the energy loss in consecutive cycles of tension. Moreover, the increase of the stretching strain from 70 % to 270 % had resulted in larger hysteresis loop area with the dissipated energy and the energy loss ratio increasing from 17.79 kJ/m3 to 544.86 kJ/m3 and 44.61 % to 77.83 %, respectively. This phenomenon could be explained in the fact that the crosslinking network of CDs and DACNF connected by hydrogen bonds preferentially was broken to dissipate energy when the composite hydrogel was subjected to small-strain stretching. As the strain gradually increased, the ether bond formed by between PVA and DACNF was destroyed by an efficient energy dissipation mechanism, thus achieving a higher energy loss ratio. Though the dissipated energy increased with the augment of strain, the final energy loss after 4 tensile cycles remained basically unchanged, which confirmed excellent fatigue resistance of PDC hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 3e, several hysteresis loops also appeared during 50 cyclic loading-unloading processes. And the dissipated energy was significantly decreased from 27.18 kJ/m3 to 8.04 kJ/m3 during 50 cyclic processes until it stabilized (Fig. 3f). This result revealed that PDC hydrogel possessed good fatigue resistance against the repeated deformations.

As shown in Fig. 4a, dynamic covalent-borate ester bond was formed by strong complexation between borate ions and adjacent hydroxyl groups of PVA under alkaline conditions, fixing the temporary shape of the composite hydrogel. The PD hydrogel could be programmed into the desirable temporary shape “U” after the as-prepared strip hydrogel was deformed by external force and immersed into borax solution at room temperature. The borax-triggered shape memory test was conducted within 10 min to determine the effect of different DACNF addition on shape memory properties. From Fig. 4b, it is clearly observed that the shape fixity ratio decreased from 97 % at 0.5 wt% DACNF addition to 73 % at 2.0 wt%. The reason mainly attributed to increased crosslink density of hydrogel network to further enhance hardness of PD hydrogel. However, with the increase of the shape memory time from 2 to 10 min, the shape fixity ratio raised up to 82 % with 1.5 wt% DACNF addition (Fig. 4c). Additionally, owing to the break of dynamic borate ester bonds, the hydrogel with 1.5 wt% DACNF addition could be recovered to the original shape after being treated in hydrochloric acid solution (pH = 4) within several minutes (Fig. 4d). Therefore, for the balance of mechanical strength and shape memory properties, the hydrogel with 1.5 wt% addition of DACNF was selected as a typical example for further study.

Based on shape memory properties endowed by the dynamic borate ester bonds and the good mechanical properties, 2D hydrogels could be modularly assembled to construct interesting 3D geometric structure. As shown in Fig. 5a, five pieces of pentagonal PD hydrogel sheets were first spliced together by hands, and then treated with borax solution for a while. Due to the formation of dynamic borate ester bonds, PD hydrogel sheets self-healed into a planar polygon. As expected, the spliced polygon could be soon reverted to 2D hydrogel pieces in acid solution owing to the break of dynamic borate ester bonds. Furthermore, a tailored 2D hydrogel sheet could be spliced and folded into 3D geometry. As illustrated in Fig. 5b–c, 2D hydrogel sheets cut into special shapes were first bent by hands, and then treated with borax solution to form a 3D hydrogel bowl or pinwheel by triggering self-healing mechanism. Similarly, the bowl-shaped and pinwheel-shaped hydrogels also were recovered to initial state in acid solution displaying good shape memory property. Furthermore, it is possible to constructed more complex 3D hydrogel structure (e.g., crane-shaped hydrogel shown in Fig. 5d) based on reversible shape memory and recovery. The PD hydrogel also exhibited the cyclicity of shape memory. As illustrated in Fig. 5e, the strip hydrogel was fixed into triangle and “W” shape after two cycles of shape memory respectively, showing good durability. It is an interesting finding as complex 3D structures could be constructed by simply splicing and folding customized 2D hydrogel samples. Subsequently, to investigate the influence of shape memory cycles on the mechanical properties, experiment of uniaxial extension for PD hydrogel samples repeatedly immersed alternately in borax and acid solution was carried out. As shown in Fig. S6 (Supporting information), the stress and strain of these hydrogel were found to decrease from 0.92 MPa to 0.32 MPa and 507 % to 285 % with increasing shape memory cycles (from 0 to 3) due to larger apertures and more loose structure, respectively (Fig. S7, Supporting information).

The PDC hydrogel was endowed good fluorescence properties attributed to the presence of carbon quantum dots. The effect of different pH values on the fluorescence emission intensity of the PDC hydrogel at 360 nm was investigated in Fig. 6a. It could be observed that the emission intensity of PDC hydrogel obviously changed within different pH values, showing typical pH-dependent fluorescent characteristics. As displayed, the fluorescence emission intensity of PDC hydrogel remarkably increased with pH values from 2 to 7. The low emission intensity in acid solutions could be ascribed to the reason that functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs became positively charged due to protonation, reducing the excited radiation transition efficiency of CDs [28]. With the increase of pH value, the surface functional groups of CDs gradually deprotonated, resulting in the maximum fluorescence emission intensity of the hydrogel at pH = 7. Nevertheless, the intensity gradually weakened when pH value further increased to 11. It is worth noting that its downtrend of fluorescence emission intensity was slower than that of acidic solutions, indicating higher alkali resistance of carbon quantum dots compared with acid resistance. In addition, due to the complexation between Fe3+ ions and the surface functional groups of CDs, excited electrons transferred from CDs orbital to Fe3+ when hydrogels absorbed excitation energy, forming non-radiative electron/hole recombination and leading to fluorescence quenching phenomenon [29]. Based on Fe3+-controlled fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence intensity of PDC hydrogel decreased obviously (Fig. 6b). Therefore, high-contrast information could be formed by the diffusion of Fe3+ ions in the fluorescent hydrogels as a fluorescence closed switch.

Based on visible Fe3+-controlled fluorescence quenching phenomenon, data could be written onto hydrogel films for storage using Fe3+ ions and PDC fluorescent hydrogel as “ink” and “paper”, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7a, PDC fluorescent hydrogel presented pale blue color under UV light different from yellow color under natural light. The result might be attributed to photoluminescence caused by band gap transition and surface defect state transition of CDs [30,31]. Subsequently, the filter paper soaked with Fe3+ ions and cut into “CN” characters and a special logo was in contact with the fluorescent hydrogel (Fig. 7c–d). The fluorescence of CDs was quenched by Fe3+ ions to form high-contrast fluorescence quenching pattern, thus facilely printed the required information onto 2D hydrogel film. Obviously, these encrypted messages were not visible with naked eye in natural light expect under UV light (Fig. 7b). However, the encrypted information coded in 2D fluorescent hydrogel films could be read by simple UV light illumination, making the fluorescence-based 2D data encrypted method less security.

To improve encoding security, this work constructs a complex 3D spatial encryption method by combining shape memory and fluorescence switching actions. As shown in Fig. 8a, one 2D fluorescent hydrogel film with “CNFU” letters was first printed by Fe3+ ion printing. After that, the hydrogel film was folded into a triangular pyramid by hands and then immersed into borax solution for 30 s. The 3D triangular pyramid hydrogel structure was fixed due to the formation of dynamic borate ester bonds between PVA and borax. The “CNFU” data were successfully encoded inside the 3D hydrogel geometry by a simple Fe3+ ion printing method and the formation of borate ester bonds. Following a similar strategy, the “1C2H3Z” information was encrypted into a 3D cube-shaped hydrogel structure (Fig. 8b). Obviously, it was difficult to observe the encrypted information clearly under either 365 nm UV light or natural light. Therefore, the 3D hydrogel geometries required a separate acid-triggered shape recovery process to decrypt the loaded data (Fig. 8c). The borate ester bonds generated originally were broken and the shape of the fluorescent hydrogel was restored by immersing in acid environment. In addition, if these hydrogel geometries were not unfolded and decrypted according to the predefined steps, incorrect information would be presented. Since shape recovery conditions are unknown to the public, this 3D information encryption method provides a higher security level than traditional 2D information encryption.

Researchers have designed a series of methods to recycle paper and ink in order to reduce the energy consumption and environmental pollution. Similar to other reusable materials, the as- prepared fluorescent hydrogels could be recovered by ascorbic acid treatment for data erasure. The strong reducing ascorbic acid could reduce Fe3+ ions to Fe2+ ions from the surface of CDs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence properties and the erasure of information. As shown in Fig. 9a, the Chinese characters “北京” was successfully loaded into the hydrogel film by ion-printing method based on fluorescence quenching between Fe3+ ions and CDs. Then, the information presented under UV light gradually disappeared after being treated with 0.2 mol/L ascorbic acid solution as “eraser”, causing the recovery of the fluorescence property. Moreover, the date erasure of fluorescent hydrogel could be achieved within 3 min, demonstrating fast fluorescence recovery after being treated with ascorbic acid solution. It is noted that the fluorescent hydrogel should be washed thoroughly to remove ascorbic acid and Fe2+ ions after treatment. Subsequently, the storage and erase of “I♥U” hidden information on fluorescence hydrogel were conducted in the same way (Fig. 9b). Therefore, we believed that the alternate use of Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid solution could not only enhance data security by destroying the decrypted information timely, but also realize the recycling of “gel paper” to reduce resource consumption.

In summary, a fluorescent hydrogel with desirable mechanical property and borax-triggered shape memory was successfully developed through sodium periodate oxidation, one-step hydrothermal method and acetal reaction. The materials were based on DACNF, N-doped CDs and PVA hydrogel, endowed with Fe3+-controlled fluorescence quenching, as well as borax-triggered shape memory and ascorbic acid fluorescence recovery. The synthesized CDs with a diameter of 2–6 nm were amorphous particles without continuous and complete lattice. The UV–Vis absorption peaks of CDs were 350 nm and 240 nm, and the optimal fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths were 360 nm and 450 nm, respectively, showing obvious photoluminescence phenomenon. In addition, DACNF was introduced into the hydrogel acted as chemical crosslinking agent and reinforcer, improving the mechanical property but with the decreased of shape memory. The leap of data encryption from 2D plane to 3D space was realized by folding and assembling 2D hydrogel based on Fe3+-controlled fluorescence quenching and shape memory triggered by borax. Moreover, the decryption of the data information needed to be interpreted in a predetermined way. It is noted that the quenching fluorescence properties could be restored to achieve the erasing of encrypted data in view of strong reduction of ascorbic acid. Compared with traditional 2D encryption mode, this facile and general of the 3D counterpart possessed superior encryption security.
Sijie Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing-Original draft preparation.
Han Zhong: Data curation, Software.
Zhiguo Wang: Visualization, Investigation.
Haifeng Lv: Software, Validation.
Jianchun Jiang and Junwen Pu: Conceptualization, Writing-Reviewing and Editing.
All authors commented on the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by the Scientific Research Funds of Huaqiao University (605-50Y22069), and Special Fund for Beijing Common Construction Project, Grant No. 2016HXKFCLXY0015.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.128359.
